name: Edinburgh, City of Literature
title: Writers, portraits, places & readings
note: Portraits and places for each writer, a few short films, and read-aloud passages from the classics. Tap a chapter to jump to that author's story.

items.caption: Lady Stair's House on the Royal Mile, now the Writers' Museum, dedicated to Burns, Scott and Stevenson. Edinburgh was the world's first UNESCO City of Literature.
items.kidcaption: This old house on the Royal Mile is a museum all about Edinburgh's most famous writers. The city was the first ever named a 'City of Literature'.

items.caption: Robert Burns, Scotland's national poet. He wrote in Scots, and gave the world Auld Lang Syne and A Red, Red Rose, sung by millions who have no idea they are his.
items.kidcaption: Robert Burns, Scotland's most famous poet. You already know one of his songs: everyone sings Auld Lang Syne at New Year!

items.title: Auld Lang Syne, by Robert Burns
items.caption: A Scots performance of Burns's Auld Lang Syne, the song the whole world sings on New Year's Eve.
items.kidcaption: Listen to the real Scottish song you sing at New Year, written by Robert Burns.

items.title: Reading: A Red, Red Rose (Burns)
items.caption: A short reading of Burns's most famous love poem.
items.kidcaption: A reading of Burns's famous poem, A Red, Red Rose.
items.credit: Read aloud (Kokoro TTS); text is public domain
items.license: 'A Red, Red Rose' by Robert Burns (1794) — public domain

items.caption: Sir Walter Scott, who all but invented the historical novel (Waverley, Ivanhoe, Rob Roy) and was the most famous writer in the world in his day.
items.kidcaption: Sir Walter Scott, who wrote thrilling adventure stories about the past, like Ivanhoe and Rob Roy. He was a superstar author 200 years ago.

items.caption: The Scott Monument on Princes Street, a 200-foot Gothic rocket of a memorial, often called the largest monument to a writer anywhere. Edinburgh's Waverley Station is named after his novel.
items.kidcaption: The giant black rocket-shaped tower is a monument to one writer, Walter Scott. Even the city's main train station, Waverley, is named after his book!

items.title: Reading: The Lady of the Lake (Scott)
items.caption: The opening lines of Scott's narrative poem set in the Trossachs, near Stirling.
items.kidcaption: The stag-at-dawn opening of one of Walter Scott's most famous poems.
items.credit: Read aloud (Kokoro TTS); text is public domain
items.license: 'The Lady of the Lake' by Sir Walter Scott (1810) — public domain

items.caption: Robert Louis Stevenson, Edinburgh-born author of Treasure Island, Kidnapped, and Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde.
items.kidcaption: Robert Louis Stevenson, who wrote Treasure Island, the pirate story that gave us treasure maps, peg-legs and 'X marks the spot'.

items.caption: An illustration from Treasure Island. Stevenson set the template for every pirate story since: the map, the black spot, the one-legged Long John Silver.
items.kidcaption: A picture from Treasure Island. Almost everything you picture about pirates, the map, the parrot, 'X marks the spot', comes from this one book.

items.caption: Deacon Brodie's Tavern. The real Brodie was a respectable craftsman by day and a burglar by night, hanged in 1788, and a likely spark for Jekyll and Hyde.
items.kidcaption: This pub is named after Deacon Brodie, a real man who was a respectable gentleman by day and a sneaky burglar by night. Sound like anyone? Jekyll and Hyde!

items.title: Treasure Island — animated summary
items.caption: A short animated tour of Stevenson's Treasure Island, the book that invented the pirate story as we know it.
items.kidcaption: A quick cartoon of the whole Treasure Island story: the map, the pirates, and Long John Silver.

items.title: Reading: Treasure Island (Stevenson)
items.caption: The pirate song and the arrival of the old sea-dog, from the opening of Treasure Island.
items.kidcaption: 'Fifteen men on the dead man's chest...' the pirate song that opens Treasure Island.
items.credit: Read aloud (Kokoro TTS); text is public domain
items.license: 'Treasure Island' by Robert Louis Stevenson (1883) — public domain

items.caption: Arthur Conan Doyle, born in Edinburgh in 1859, creator of Sherlock Holmes. The model for Holmes was his university teacher Dr Joseph Bell.
items.kidcaption: Arthur Conan Doyle, who invented Sherlock Holmes. He based the great detective on his real teacher, a doctor who could guess a stranger's job at a glance.

items.caption: Sherlock Holmes as first drawn by Sidney Paget. Detective fiction as we know it was essentially born in an Edinburgh classroom.
items.kidcaption: The very first pictures of Sherlock Holmes looked like this. The whole idea of a brilliant detective solving clues started right here in Edinburgh.

items.title: Dr Joseph Bell — the real-life Sherlock Holmes
items.caption: The story of Dr Joseph Bell, the Edinburgh surgeon whose uncanny powers of observation gave Conan Doyle his Sherlock Holmes.
items.kidcaption: Meet the real doctor whose amazing observation skills gave the world Sherlock Holmes.

items.title: Reading: A Study in Scarlet (Doyle)
items.caption: Holmes's very first deduction about Dr Watson, from the first Sherlock Holmes story.
items.kidcaption: Sherlock's very first magic trick: guessing all about Watson the moment they meet.
items.credit: Read aloud (Kokoro TTS); text is public domain
items.license: 'A Study in Scarlet' by Arthur Conan Doyle (1887) — public domain

items.caption: The Elephant House cafe, one of the Edinburgh cafes where J.K. Rowling wrote parts of early Harry Potter. (She wrote in several places; enjoy the legend with a pinch of salt.)
items.kidcaption: This cafe is one of the places J.K. Rowling sat and wrote Harry Potter, looking out at the castle. Imagine dreaming up Hogwarts over a cup of hot chocolate!

items.caption: Greyfriars Kirkyard, a short walk away. Fans love to point out old gravestones here (including a 'Thomas Riddell') as Harry Potter inspiration, though Rowling has never confirmed it.
items.kidcaption: This spooky old graveyard has a gravestone reading 'Thomas Riddell'. Harry Potter fans think it inspired a certain villain's name. Nobody knows for sure!

items.caption: George Heriot's School, an old turreted school beside Greyfriars that fans like to compare to Hogwarts, with its four houses. A fun 'maybe', not a confirmed fact.
items.kidcaption: Does this turreted old school look a bit like Hogwarts to you? It even has four houses! Fans think it gave Rowling ideas, but it is just a fun maybe.

items.title: Harry Potter in Edinburgh — the inspiration spots
items.caption: A walk round the Edinburgh places fans link to Harry Potter, from the cafes to Greyfriars and Victoria Street.
items.kidcaption: See the real Edinburgh streets and graveyards that fans think inspired Harry Potter.
